edges.rel: promised to incarnate as son of

beats.title: Invocation
beats.summary: The chapter begins with an invocation to Narayana, Nara, Narottama, Devi Sarasvati, and Vyasa, followed by the declaration of victory (jaya).

beats.title: The Beginning of Vamana Purana
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about the Vamana Purana, specifically about Vishnu's dwarf incarnation, Prahlada's war with the gods, and Sati's story. Pulastya begins narrating the Purana, starting with Sati's complaint to Shiva about lack of shelter during summer and rains, leading to Shiva taking residence on a cloud, earning the name Jimutaketu.

beats.title: Chapter 2
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Nārada the story of Dakṣa's sacrifice, Śiva's exclusion, and the origin of Śiva's kapāla (skull) due to a conflict with Brahmā. The chapter describes the cosmic creation, the battle between Brahmā's created being and Śiva, and the birth of Skanda from Nārāyaṇa's arm.

beats.title: Chapter 3
beats.summary: Rudra (Shiva) commits brahmahatyā (killing a Brahmin) and is pursued by the sin personified. He seeks purification at various tīrthas but fails. Finally, he prays to Viṣṇu, who instructs him to go to Vārāṇasī and bathe in the Kapālamocana tīrtha, where his sin is removed and he becomes known as Kapālī.

beats.title: The Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Sati, daughter of Daksha, is not invited to Daksha's sacrifice because her husband Shiva is a kapali (beggar). She learns this from Jayā and dies of grief. Shiva, enraged, creates Virabhadra and Bhadrakali from his body. Virabhadra attacks the sacrifice, fights with Dharma and other gods, and is eventually subdued by Vishnu. Shiva then enters the sacrifice himself.

beats.title: Shiva's Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice and the Revelation of His Cosmic Form
beats.summary: Pulastya describes to Narada how Shiva, in his Kala-rupa (Time-form), destroyed Daksha's sacrifice after being enraged by the gods' disrespect. The chapter details the cosmic body of Shiva as the zodiac and constellations, and then describes the characteristics of the twelve zodiac signs.

beats.title: The Fall of Shiva's Linga and the Search for Its End
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Nara-Narayana's penance in Badarikashrama, the attempt by Indra to disturb them by sending Kamadeva and spring, the subsequent burning of Kamadeva by Shiva after Sati's self-immolation, Shiva's madness and wandering, his encounter with the yaksha Pañcalika, the curse by sages causing Shiva's linga to fall, and the search for its end by Brahma and Vishnu.

beats.title: The Origin of Śaiva Traditions and the Burning of Kāma
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of the Śaiva, Pāśupata, Kālavadana, and Kāpālika traditions, the establishment of Śiva's liṅga in Citravana, and the burning of Kāma (Madana) by Śiva's third eye, leading to the creation of various fragrant trees and plants.

beats.title: The Battle of Nara-Narayana and Prahlada
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about the story of Nara-Narayana and Prahlada. Pulastya narrates how the sage Cyavana was taken to the netherworld by a serpent, met Prahlada, and told him about the Naimisha forest. Prahlada led the Danavas to Naimisha to bathe and see Vishnu. There, he encountered two sages (Nara and Narayana) with bows, challenged them, and a fierce battle ensued. After exchanging many arrows and divine weapons, Prahlada attacked with a mace, but Narayana stood firm.

beats.title: The Battle and Surrender of Prahlada
beats.summary: Prahlada battles Narayana (as Sādhyā) for a thousand divine years, is unable to defeat him, then learns from Pītavāsā (a form of Vishnu) that victory comes through devotion, not war. Prahlada surrenders, receives boons, renounces his kingdom, and becomes a yogi devoted to Vishnu.

beats.title: Chapter 9
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya how Andhaka, born blind, was crowned king by Prahlada. Pulastya explains that Andhaka regained sight after Hiranyaksha's death and was installed by Prahlada. Andhaka then performed penance to Shiva, obtained boons of invincibility, and ruled with Shukra as priest. He attacked the gods, conquered the earth, and marched on Mount Meru. Indra assembled the gods, and a great battle ensued between devas and asuras. The chapter describes the vehicles of various deities and demons, the chaotic battle, and the river of blood.

beats.title: The Battle of the Gods and Andhaka
beats.summary: The chapter describes a fierce battle between the gods and the demons (daityas), led by Andhaka. Indra, Yama, Varuna, Soma, Agni, and other deities fight against Andhaka, Prahlada, Virocana, Jambha, Maya, and others. Andhaka defeats many gods, forcing them to retreat, and ultimately enters the city of Ashmaka in Patala.

beats.title: Sukeśin's Fall to Pātāla
beats.summary: Narada continues his narration, describing how the demon Sukeśin, after being thrown from the sky by Vishnu, fell to Pātāla and settled there with the Apsaras for his service.

beats.title: Sukeśi's Inquiry about Dharma and the Geography of the Continents
beats.summary: The chapter begins with Pulastya narrating to an audience the story of the demon Sukeśi, son of Vidyutkeśa. Sukeśi receives a boon from Śiva granting him an aerial city and invincibility. He later visits a hermitage of sages in the Magadha forest and asks them about the supreme good (śreyas) in this world and the next. The sages explain that dharma is the highest good, and then describe the specific dharmas for different classes of beings: gods, demons, siddhas, gandharvas, vidyādharas, kiṃpuruṣas, pitṛs, rākṣasas, piśācas, and humans. Sukeśi then asks for more details about human dharma. The sages proceed to describe the geography of the seven continents (dvīpas) and the oceans, starting from Jambūdvīpa up to Puṣkaradvīpa, and explain the dharmas of the inhabitants of each. Sukeśi questions why Puṣkaradvīpa is described as terrible and associated with hells. The sages begin to describe the hells, starting with Raurava.

beats.title: Enumeration of Hells
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates various hells (narakas) and their descriptions, including Andhatamisraka, Kalacakra, Apratistha, Ghatiyantra, Asipatravana, Taptakumbha, Kutasalmali, Karapatra, Svanabhojana, Samdamsa, Lohapinda, Karambhasikata, Ksaranadi, Krmibhojana, Vaitarani, Sonitapuyabhojana, Ksuragradhara, Cakraka, Samsosana, and Ananta. The chapter ends with Sukeśin asking about the karmas that lead to these hells.

beats.title: Description of Hells and the Supremacy of Various Entities
beats.summary: The sages describe various hells (narakas) that sinners fall into based on their specific misdeeds, such as disrespecting parents, teachers, and sacred beings; stealing; betraying trust; and engaging in forbidden acts. The chapter also extols the supremacy of certain entities and practices, culminating in the declaration that the hell Vaitaraṇī is the most dreadful for sinners.

beats.title: The Inexpiable Sin of Ingratitude
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the absence of expiation for the ungrateful, even over billions of years, and concludes with Sukeśi's remark about the terrifying nature of Puṣkaradvīpa.

beats.title: Geography of Jambudvipa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vamana Purana describes the geography of Jambudvipa, focusing on its nine divisions (varshas), the central Ilavrita, and the surrounding regions. It details the mountains, rivers, and various peoples inhabiting the land, particularly emphasizing the Bharata varsha with its nine islands, seven main mountain ranges, and numerous rivers. The text lists many tribes and kingdoms, both Aryan and Mleccha, and notes the spiritual significance of the rivers.

beats.title: Description of Regions and Virtues
beats.summary: This part of Chapter 13 of the Vamana Purana lists various regions (viṣayāḥ) in the Kumara Dvipa, including Kuthapravarana, Urna, Punya, Sahuhuka, Trigarta, Kirata, Tomara, and Sisira-adrika. It then introduces a dialogue where the sages (ṛṣayaḥ) speak about virtues such as non-violence (ahiṃsā), truthfulness (satya), charity (dāna), forgiveness (kṣānti), self-control (dama), and tranquility (śama).

beats.title: Sukeśi Learns About Dharma and Sadācāra
beats.summary: The chapter begins with the demon Sukeśi asking sages about the four āśramas and the duties of a householder. The sages explain the ten-limbed dharma (ahiṃsā, satya, etc.) and describe the brahmacārī's life, then the householder's duties including marriage, earning wealth, and performing rituals. They emphasize the importance of sadācāra (good conduct) and detail daily routines: waking in brahma muhūrta, reciting suprabhātam, bodily purification, sandhyā worship, and various prohibitions regarding eating, sleeping, and social interactions. The chapter concludes with advice on living in a righteous kingdom.

beats.title: Qualities of a Virtuous Land
beats.summary: The sages describe the qualities of a virtuous land: where people are free from anger, devoted to justice, without envy, and where farmers and herbs thrive. They advise that a wise king should not dwell in lands where the ruler is fond of punishment, nor should anyone live among people who are constantly quarrelsome and aggressive.

beats.title: Rules of Purity and the Householder's Dharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vamana Purana discusses rules of purity and impurity (śauca) regarding food, objects, and persons, as well as duties of householders and the transition to the forest-dwelling stage. It includes a dialogue between sages and Sukeśi, defining various types of 'unfit' persons and the penances for contact with them.

beats.title: The Duties of the Vānaprastha Āśrama and the Importance of Svadharma
beats.summary: This chapter describes the duties of the vānaprastha (forest-dwelling) āśrama, including practices such as living in the forest, performing austerities, sleeping on the ground, celibacy, worship of ancestors and gods, daily homa, bathing three times, wearing matted hair and bark garments, and using only forest products. It then discusses the appropriate āśramas for each varṇa: for brāhmaṇas and kṣatriyas, three āśramas (gārhasthya, brahmacarya, vānaprastha); for vaiśyas, two (vaikhānasa and gārhasthya); for śūdras, only gārhasthya. It emphasizes that one should not abandon one's own dharma, as the sun god (Bhāskara) becomes angry and destroys the family and increases disease. Finally, Sukeśī, having heard this from the sages, returns to his city, repeatedly contemplating dharma.

beats.title: The Fall of Sūrya and the Power of Dharma
beats.summary: Sage Pulastya narrates to Nārada how the rākṣasas under Sukeśi adopted dharma and grew powerful, causing cosmic confusion as day and night became indistinguishable. The sun god Sūrya, angered by the rākṣasas' rise, attacked their city, but it was protected by Śiva. Sūrya was then struck down by Śiva's third eye and fell, advised by sages to land in the holy field of Hari (Vārāṇasī). He fell between the rivers Varaṇā and Asi, suffering from Śiva's fire.

beats.title: The Restoration of Sukeśin and the Sun
beats.summary: Brahma, accompanied by the gods, goes to Mount Mandara to meet Shiva and pleases him. Shiva takes the sun (Surya) in his hand, names it 'Lola', and places it back on its chariot. Brahma then restores Sukeśin (the demon king) along with his city and relatives to the sky. The chapter recounts how the sun had previously caused Sukeśin's city to fall to earth, but Shiva's glance did not burn the sun; instead, Shiva lifted the sun from the ground and placed it back, and Brahma restored Sukeśin.

beats.title: The Vows of the Cosmic Sleep
beats.summary: Pulastya describes to Narada the sacred vows (vratas) and rituals associated with the cosmic sleep of deities, particularly Vishnu and Shiva, during the four-month rainy season (Cāturmāsya). The chapter details specific tithis, offerings, and procedures for worship to ensure household prosperity and avoid separation from loved ones.

beats.title: The Year-Long Vow for Shiva's Grace
beats.summary: This chapter describes the monthly rituals for worshipping Shiva (Rudra) throughout the year, specifying the bathing, offerings, incense, and mantras for each month from Jyeshtha to Ashvayuja. It concludes by stating that performing this vow for a full year grants all desires, as declared by Rudra himself.

beats.title: The Akhanda Vrata and the Origin of Mahishasura
beats.summary: This chapter of the Vamana Purana describes the origin of various sacred trees and plants from the bodies of deities, and then details the observance of the Akhanda (unbroken) vow dedicated to Vishnu, including the ritual of worship, the recitation of the Vaishnava Panjara (protective armor), and the story of the demons Rambha and Karambha and the goddess Katyayani's slaying of Mahishasura.

beats.title: The Birth and Deeds of Hayagriva and Raktabija
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the demon Hayagriva (Hayari), born from the union of the demon Malavata and the yakshini Shyama. After Malavata is killed by a buffalo, Shyama seeks refuge with the yakshas. Hayagriva, driven by desire, is stopped by the yakshas and later dies. Malavata is cremated, and Shyama immolates herself. From the fire emerges a fierce being who kills many buffaloes, sparing one. This being is identified as the demon Raktabija, who later defeats the gods. The chapter ends with the gods being defeated by Mahisha (the buffalo demon).

beats.title: The Birth of Katyayani and the Humility of Vindhya
beats.summary: The gods, defeated by the demon Mahisha, seek refuge with Vishnu and Shiva. Their combined energies, along with those of other deities, are channeled through the sage Katyayana to create the goddess Katyayani (Durga), who is then armed by the gods and sent to slay Mahisha. The chapter also includes a sub-story of how the sage Agastya made the Vindhya mountain lower its peak, allowing the sun's path, and how Durga later took residence on that peak.

beats.title: The Slaying of Mahishasura
beats.summary: The chapter describes how the demon Mahishasura, upon hearing from his messengers Chanda and Munda about the beautiful goddess Katyayani (Durga) residing on the Vindhya mountain, sends his army to capture her. The goddess demands a bride-price: she will marry only the one who defeats her in battle. Mahishasura's general Dundubhi delivers the message, and the goddess prepares for war. The gods, led by Brahma, request her to don armor, but she refuses. Instead, Lord Shiva provides the Vishnupanjara (a protective shield) for her. The chapter ends with the goddess slaying Mahishasura by stamping him with her feet.

beats.title: The Slaying of Mahiṣāsura by Kātyāyanī
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the story of Devi Kātyāyanī's battle with Mahiṣāsura and his army. The Devi, aided by her lion and various bhūtagaṇas, defeats the asura forces, ultimately slaying Mahiṣāsura after a fierce combat. The gods praise her as Nārāyaṇī, and she promises to return for the welfare of the gods.

beats.title: The Origin of the Goddess and the Love of Samvarana and Tapati
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the origin of the goddess (Kaushiki/Parvati) and the story of King Samvarana's love for Tapatī, daughter of the Sun, mediated by sage Vasiṣṭha. The chapter also includes the gods' request to Vishnu for help against Mahiṣa, leading to the plan for Parvati's birth.

beats.title: The Marriage of Saṃvaraṇa and Tapati
beats.summary: King Saṃvaraṇa, son of Ṛkṣa, arrives at Vasiṣṭha's hermitage and sees Tapati, the daughter of Sūrya (Vivasvat). Vasiṣṭha explains that he arranged for Tapati to be given to Saṃvaraṇa, and the king marries her. Tapati rejoices with her husband.

beats.title: The Plowing of Kurukshetra and the Boon of Dharmakshetra
beats.summary: King Kuru, son of Samvarana, performs a symbolic plowing of the sacred land of Kurukshetra, which becomes the Dharmakshetra. He is tested by Indra and Vishnu, who cut his limbs, but Kuru remains steadfast. Vishnu grants him a boon that the land will be a supreme holy place for dharma, and appoints guardians to protect it.

beats.title: The Sacred Tirtha Prithudaka and the Birth of Himavan's Daughters
beats.summary: The chapter describes the sacredness of the tirtha Prithudaka in Kurukshetra, where gods perform rituals to please the Pitris. It narrates how the gods, led by Indra and Brihaspati, observe an auspicious time (when the moon and sun are in Mrigashira nakshatra with Brihaspati) and perform shraddha. This leads to the birth of three daughters from the union of Himavan and Mena, who was given to Himavan by the gods.

beats.title: The Birth and Penance of Menā's Daughters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vamana Purana narrates the birth of three daughters of Menā (Rāgiṇī, Kuṭilā, and Kālī), their penances, and the curses they receive from Brahmā. Kuṭilā becomes a river, Rāgiṇī becomes Sandhyā, and Kālī (Umā) performs severe penance to win Śiva as her husband. Śiva tests her by appearing as a brahmacārin, and the chapter ends with their dialogue.

beats.title: Parvati's Penance and Shiva's Test
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the story of Parvati's penance to win Shiva as her husband. A sage (Shiva in disguise) tests her resolve, and after her unwavering devotion, Shiva reveals himself, blesses her, and establishes the Bhadreshvara linga. Parvati returns to her father's home, and Shiva goes to Pṛthūdaka for a bath, then to Mount Mandara for worship.

beats.title: Shiva's Marriage Proposal to Parvati
beats.summary: The chapter describes how Lord Shiva, after being worshipped by the mountain, summons the seven sages and Arundhati to request the hand of Parvati (Uma), the daughter of Himavan, in marriage. The sages go to Himavan's palace, convey Shiva's proposal, and Himavan consults with his family and other mountains before agreeing.

beats.title: The Marriage of Kālī and Śiva
beats.summary: Himavan consults with other mountains and sages about giving his daughter Kālī (Pārvatī) in marriage to Śiva. The sages approve, and Menā reveals a prophecy that the son born to Kālī will slay the demons Mahiṣa and Tāraka. Himavan gives Kālī to Śiva, and the sages fix an auspicious time for the wedding. The sages return to Śiva, who honors them. Later, gods including Brahmā, Viṣṇu, Indra, and Sūrya visit Śiva's abode.

beats.title: The Wedding of Shiva and Parvati
beats.summary: This chapter describes the wedding procession of Shiva and Parvati, including the gathering of gods, sages, and other beings on Mount Kailasa, the journey to Himavan's palace, the wedding ceremony, and the playful interactions during the event.

beats.title: Shiva's Marriage and Return to Mandara
beats.summary: Shiva (Rudra) marries Parvati (Draupadi's daughter? Actually, Parvati is daughter of Himavan, but text says 'draputrī' meaning daughter of Drona? Wait, 'draputrī' likely means daughter of Drona? No, context: after marriage, Shiva returns to Mandara. The chapter describes the aftermath of Shiva's marriage, including the Vālakhilya sages emerging from a crisis caused by Shiva's anger, and Shiva's return to Mount Mandara with his consort and attendants.

beats.title: The Penance of Parvati and the Birth of Katyayani
beats.summary: Rudra (Shiva) lives on a mountain with Parvati. During a playful argument, Parvati is offended when Shiva calls her 'Kali'. She leaves to perform severe penance on Himavan. A tiger waits to eat her, but she remains still for a hundred years. Brahma grants the tiger boons (devotion to Ganesha, invincibility). Parvati sheds her dark skin, becoming golden, and from that skin emerges the goddess Katyayani. Indra takes her as his sister (Kaushiki) and establishes her as Vindhyavasini. Parvati returns to Shiva. Meanwhile, Shiva and Parvati engage in prolonged lovemaking, causing cosmic disturbance. The gods send Agni to interrupt. Agni enters Shiva's abode as a swan and informs him. Shiva withdraws and releases his seed. Agni consumes it. Parvati curses the gods that their wives will be barren.

beats.title: The Birth of Vinayaka (Ganesha)
beats.summary: Goddess Gauri curses the gods and then creates a son from her body's impurities, who becomes Ganesha. The chapter narrates the birth of Vinayaka (Ganesha) from Parvati's bath water and the impurities of Shiva and Parvati, his naming, and the granting of a boon that he will be worshipped first and cause obstacles for the gods.

beats.title: The Slaying of Namuci and the Rise of Sumbha and Nisumbha
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the story of the demon Namuci who was granted invincibility by Indra but was killed by Indra using foam. Later, the demons Sumbha and Nisumbha conquer the three worlds. They encounter the demon Raktabija and his ministers Candamunda. The goddess Kausiki (Devi) is approached by Sumbha's messenger Sugriva, who demands she marry Sumbha or Nisumbha. She sets a condition: whoever defeats her in battle will be her husband. Sumbha sends Dhumralocana, who is burned to ashes by Devi's humkara. Then Sumbha sends Candamunda and Ruru with a vast army, but Devi's lion attacks them.

beats.title: The Slaying of Chanda and Munda
beats.summary: The goddess Kaushiki (Ambika) sends Kali (Chamunda) to kill the demons Chanda and Munda. Kali defeats them, brings their heads, and is blessed by Ambika to be known as Chamunda. The demon army is routed and survivors flee to Shumbha.

beats.title: The Slaying of Śumbha and Niśumbha
beats.summary: The goddess Caṇḍikā (Ambikā) defeats the demons Caṇḍa, Muṇḍa, and Raktabīja, then kills Niśumbha and Śumbha with the help of the Mātṛkās (Brahmāṇī, Māheśvarī, Kaumārī, Vaiṣṇavī, Vārāhī, Māhendrī, Nārasiṃhī) and Śiva. The chapter describes the emergence of the Mātṛkās from Caṇḍikā's body, the battle, and the final victory.

beats.title: The Goddess's Boons and Future Incarnations
beats.summary: The goddess, praised by the gods, grants boons and reveals her future incarnations to destroy demons. She then departs to the sky, and the chapter concludes with a statement of the Purana's merit.

beats.title: The Birth and Anointment of Skanda
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya how Skanda killed the demon Mahisha and the mountain Krauncha. Pulastya narrates the birth of Skanda: Shiva's seed, swallowed by Agni, passed to the river Kuṭilā, then to the Śaravaṇa forest, where Skanda was born with six faces, raised by the Kṛttikās, and later acknowledged as son by Shiva, Parvati, Kuṭilā, and Agni. The gods then proceed to Kurukshetra to anoint him as commander of the celestial army.

beats.title: Chapter 31 (part 2)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the anointment of Skanda (Karttikeya) as the commander-in-chief of the divine army, and the subsequent gifting of various gaṇas (attendants) and weapons by gods, sages, rivers, and tīrthas to Skanda.

beats.title: Skanda's Anointment and the Svastyayana
beats.summary: Kumara (Skanda) is anointed as commander of the gods' army. He seeks blessings from various deities, especially Vishnu, who performs a svastyayana (auspicious rite) for his victory over the demons Mahisha and Taraka. The demons, alarmed by the noise of the gods' army, gather under Andhaka. A battle ensues with the gods' forces, including Rudra and other ganas, attacking the demons.

beats.title: The Slaying of Taraka and the Flight of Mahisha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle between the Ganas (attendants of Shiva) led by various Gana chiefs and the Asuras led by Mahisha and Taraka. After fierce fighting, Taraka is killed by Skanda (Kartikeya) with his spear. Mahisha flees to a cave. Skanda hesitates to kill Mahisha due to familial ties, but Indra and others persuade him by citing dharma. A contest arises between Skanda and Indra over who circumambulated Mount Krauncha first, which they refer to Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva for judgment.

beats.title: The Slaying of Mahiṣāsura and the Penance of Sucakranetrā
beats.summary: Krauñca, after being addressed, speaks first; then the son of Gotrabhit (Kārttikeya) pierces Krauñca and the buffalo-demon with his spear. Sunābha, the mountain-king, arrives; Brahmā, Indra, Rudra, and others go to heaven seeing the buffalo slain by Guha. Kumāra (Skanda) attempts to uproot his spear to kill his maternal uncle, but is restrained by Viṣṇu (Cakradhara) who embraces him and calls him 'guru'. Himālaya takes Sunābha away. Hari (Viṣṇu) takes Kumāra with his peacock to heaven. Guha laments killing his maternal uncle and resolves to wither his body. Viṣṇu advises him to go to the tīrtha Pṛthūdaka, bathe in Oghavatī, and worship Hara (Śiva) to become radiant as the sun. Kumāra does so, worships Śiva, and becomes radiant. Sucakranetrā performs severe penance on a mountain, propitiating Śiva, who grants him a boon for a divine cakra to cut Bāṇa's arms. Śiva grants it. Gaṇeśvara approaches Skanda and announces Śiva's grace. The chapter concludes with the killing of Mahiṣāsura by the spear of Śiva's son, and the death of Krauñca for the sake of the refugee, which removes sin and increases merit.

beats.title: The Slaying of Patalaketu and the Infatuation of Andhaka
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya how the demon Patalaketu was killed. Pulastya narrates the story of King Ritadhvaja, who killed Patalaketu to protect the sage Galava's penance. Then, after the deaths of Taraka and Mahisha, Andhaka becomes enraged and attacks the gods. He sees Parvati (Gauri) and becomes infatuated. Despite Prahlada's warnings that she is the wife of Shiva, Andhaka pursues her. Nandi defends her but is struck down. Parvati transforms into Shatarupa and disappears. Andhaka's forces are routed, and he retreats to the underworld, tormented by desire.

beats.title: Shiva's Penance and the Demon Mura
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about Shiva's whereabouts during the battle with Andhaka. Pulastya narrates that after a thousand years of delusion, Shiva became weak and undertook severe penance. He wandered the earth as a mendicant, and while meditating, his matted lock fell and created the Kedara tirtha. Shiva blessed Kedara, then bathed in the Yamuna and Sarasvati rivers. While submerged in Sarasvati, the universe began to shake. The gods approached Brahma, who directed them to Vishnu. Meanwhile, the demon Mura, son of Kashyapa and Danu, had obtained a boon from Brahma that anyone he touched with his hand would die. He conquered heaven, forcing Indra to flee. Mura then challenged King Raghu, but sage Vasistha advised him to conquer Yama instead. Yama, unable to fight Mura, sought Vishnu's help. Vishnu told Yama to go to Mura, and the chapter ends with Mura demanding Yama cease his duties.

beats.title: Mura's Challenge and the Four Forms of Vishnu
beats.summary: Mura challenges Yama, learns that Vishnu is his controller, and goes to the Ocean of Milk to fight him. Narada asks Pulastya about Vishnu's four forms, leading to a discussion of the four Kumaras, Sanatkumara's quest for yoga knowledge from Brahma, and the distinction between a son and a disciple.

beats.title: The Classification of Sins and the Importance of Sons
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vamana Purana discusses the classification of sins and their corresponding hells, the importance of sons and disciples for liberation from sin, and the story of Sādhya seeking yoga from Brahmā. It details twelve types of sons and their rights to inheritance, and concludes with Brahmā teaching Sanatkumāra the twelve-petalled yoga.

beats.title: The Twelve-Petalled Lotus and the Slaying of Mura
beats.summary: This chapter describes the twelve-petalled lotus form of Vishnu, correlating each petal with a letter, zodiac sign, and month. It then narrates the slaying of the demon Mura by Vishnu, who thereby becomes known as Murari. The chapter also mentions other forms of Vishnu: the four-faced Brahma (sattvic), the thousand-headed Shesha (tamasic), and the four-faced red-colored creator (rajasic).

beats.title: The Earth's Trembling and the Revelation of Shiva's Linga
beats.summary: The gods, led by Indra, approach Vishnu to complain about the earth trembling. Vishnu reveals that the cause is Shiva's severe penance and the sage Mankanaka's ecstatic dance. Vishnu instructs the gods to perform a taptakṛcchra penance and ritual bathing of a linga to purify themselves. After the penance, Vishnu shows them the linga within himself, and they worship it. The gods then go to Shiva, who is immersed in water, and request him to abandon his great vow. Shiva agrees, but the earth continues to tremble. Shiva investigates and finds the sage Shukra (Ushanas) performing penance to obtain the saṃjīvanī vidyā. Shiva grants the boon. Still the earth trembles, so Shiva goes to the Sarasvati river and finds the sage Mankanaka dancing joyfully because a drop of plant sap from his cut finger delighted him. Shiva shows him that ash from his own finger is superior, and Mankanaka stops dancing. Shiva blesses him and declares the Sarasvati tirtha sacred.

beats.title: Maṅkaṇaka's Attainment and Śiva's Return
beats.summary: The seven Sarasvatis are described as always residing and granting the fruit of the Soma sacrifice. The sage Maṅkaṇaka, instructed by Śiva, establishes a great form in Kurukṣetra and attains the Brahmaloka. After his departure, the earth becomes steady, and Śiva returns to his abode on Mandara. The chapter concludes with Nārada speaking.

beats.title: The Tale of Andhaka and the Warnings of Lust
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about Andhaka's actions in Patala and Shiva's activities on Mandara. Pulastya narrates that Andhaka, tormented by lust for Parvati, is advised by Prahlada that Parvati is his mother and Shiva his father. Prahlada recounts the story of Andhaka's birth from Shiva's third eye and the curse that he will be killed when he desires the world's mother. He also tells the cautionary tale of King Danda who lusted after Shukra's daughter Araja and was cursed, and the story of Suratha and Citrangada who married despite her father's curse.

beats.title: The Curse and Penance of Citrangada
beats.summary: The sage Danda narrates the story of Citrangada, daughter of Visvakarma, who was cursed by a sage to become a monkey-faced being due to her father's misdeed. She later performs penance at Saptagodavara, where she is promised reunion with her husband when three other women (Devavati, Nandayanti, Vedavati) arrive. The sage writes a verse declaring that no one can remove her suffering.

beats.title: The Tale of Nandayanti and the Cursed Viśvakarmā
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Nandayanti, daughter of the Guhyaka king Anjana, who is separated from her father and ends up in a forest. She meets the sage Jābāli, who is bound to a banyan tree by a monkey (Viśvakarmā in monkey form). Jābāli advises her to go to Śrīkaṇṭha (Śiva) on the Yamunā riverbank. Nandayanti follows the advice, encounters the sage Mudgala's prophecy that she will become a queen, and eventually finds the hermitage where Devavatī (the daughter of the demon Kandara) is staying.

beats.title: The Rescue of Jābāli
beats.summary: A sage named Ṛtadhvaja, after worshipping Śiva, goes to Ayodhyā to seek King Ikshvāku's help in freeing his son, who was bound by a monkey (kapi) in a banyan tree. The king sends his son Śakuni, who with great archery skill cuts the branches and frees the sage's son Jābāli. They then proceed to the river Yamunā.

beats.title: The Meeting at Pushkara
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the meeting of two yaksha daughters with sage Galava, their journey to Pushkara, and the encounter with various female beings including Citrangada and Vedavati, who share their stories of love, curse, and suffering. The narrative weaves together multiple characters and their interconnected fates.

beats.title: The Search for Lost Women and the Monkey's Boon
beats.summary: King Indradyumna, son of Manu, meets sage Ṛtadhvaja and his son Śakuni. They search for lost women, encounter a monkey (Viśvakarmā in disguise), and Ṛtadhvaja grants a boon to lift a curse.

beats.title: The Curse of the Gatekeeper
beats.summary: The chapter recounts a curse by a sage that turns someone into a gatekeeper, and the person acknowledges many sins committed.

beats.title: The Curse and the Prophecy
beats.summary: Rithadhvaja predicts that the curse on the monkey king will end when he begets a son from Ghritachi. The monkey king, delighted, descends into the great river to bathe. After bathing and worshipping the ancestors and deities, they joyfully depart on their chariots, while Ghritachi flies up to heaven, followed swiftly by the monkey king.

beats.title: Hanuman and Ghritachi
beats.summary: In this chapter, the monkey chief Hanuman sees the celestial nymph Ghritachi, who is attracted to him. Recognizing him as Visvakarman, she desires him.

beats.title: The Reunion at Hāṭakeśvara and the Birth of Nala
beats.summary: In this chapter, the narrative follows the reunion of various characters at the holy site of Hāṭakeśvara on the Godāvarī river. Key events include the recognition of past relationships, the arrival of the monkey (kapi) with Ghṛtācī, the summoning of various beings by Ṛtadhvaja, the birth of Nala to Citrāṅgadā, the liberation of Viśvakarmā from his monkey curse, and the subsequent marriage arrangements for the daughters.

beats.title: Marriage of Suratha and Citrāṅgadā
beats.summary: The chapter describes the marriage of King Suratha to Citrāṅgadā, the daughter of a Brahmin, after a series of marriages of other kings to various celestial maidens. The sage Gālava performs a sacrifice, and the gods, including Indra, are present. After the marriage, the sage advises the gods to stay at the Saptagodāvara tīrtha during the month of Mādhava. The gods agree and return to heaven. The kings take their wives and go to their cities. The story is narrated by Arajā to a lady.

beats.title: The Tale of Danda's Curse and Andhaka's Challenge
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Danda, who violated Arajā, the daughter of sage Śukra, and was cursed to be destroyed by a rain of stones. It then shifts to the demon Andhaka, who desires Pārvatī and sends Śambara to challenge Śiva. Pārvatī proposes a gambling match to decide the outcome.

beats.title: Andhaka's March to War
beats.summary: The demon Andhaka, incited by Duryodhana's drum, assembles a vast army of asuras including Jambha, Kujambha, Huṇḍa, Tuhuṇḍa, Śambara, Bali, Bāṇa, Kārtasvara, Hasti, Sūryaśatru, Mahodara, Ayaḥśuṅku, Śibi, Śālva, Vṛṣaparvā, Virocana, Hayagrīva, Kālanemi, Saṃhlāda, Kālanāśana, Śarabha, Śalabha, Vipracitti, Duryodhana, Pāka, Vipāka, Kāla, and Śambara, and marches to Mount Mandara to fight Śiva. Meanwhile, Śiva, seeing Śambara depart, summons Nandin.

beats.title: Shiva's Revelation of Non-Duality to the Gaṇas
beats.summary: Shiva (Maheshvara) summons his gaṇas through Nandi, who enumerates various classes of divine beings: Rudras, Skanda and his hosts, Pramathas, Śaivas, Pāśupatas, etc. Shiva then explains that he and Vishnu are one, and rebukes the gaṇas for their ignorance in criticizing Vishnu. He instructs them to cut his own body with a saw as a test of devotion, and finally reveals his cosmic form (Sadāśiva) encompassing all deities, leading to the purification and enlightenment of the gaṇas.

beats.title: Shiva's Boon to the Ganas and the Arrival of Andhaka
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates that the Ganas, afflicted by the sin of looking at Shiva with a split gaze, ask for a boon to remove that sin. Shiva grants it, embraces them, and they become pure. The chapter describes the scene of Shiva with his Ganas on Mount Mandara, and then introduces the arrival of Andhaka with his demons.

beats.title: Shiva's Battle Against Andhaka
beats.summary: Shiva prepares to fight the demon Andhaka. His son Vinayaka (Ganesha) and other Ganas engage the demon army. After initial setbacks, Nandi and other Gana leaders defeat the demons, including Bali, Kujambha, and Duryodhana. The demon army is routed.

beats.title: The Battle with Shambara
beats.summary: This chapter describes a fierce battle between the sons of Shiva (Kumara and his associates) and the demon Shambara and his forces. Shambara's son Ayaḥśiras fights with Viśākha, but is defeated by Naigameya and Śākha. Shambara's magical noose is destroyed by the four sons of Shiva, and Shambara flees. The demon army, terrified, seeks refuge with Shukra.

beats.title: Shukra's Capture and Boon
beats.summary: Andhako, seeing his army slain by the Pramathas, approaches Shukra for help. Shukra uses the Mrita-Sanjivani vidya to revive the slain demons. Nandi informs Shiva, who orders Shukra to be brought. Nandi is attacked by Hayakandhara but defeats him with a vajra. Kujambha, Jambha, Bala, Vritra, and Tvayasira attack Nandi. The gods, led by Brahma, come to aid. Nandi captures Shukra and brings him to Shiva, who swallows him. Shukra praises Shiva and is granted a boon to exit through the penis, becoming Shukra (the planet). He returns to the demons, and a great battle ensues with various duels.

beats.title: The Battle of Devas and Asuras and the Deception of Andhaka
beats.summary: The chapter describes a fierce battle between the gods (devas) and demons (asuras), focusing on the exploits of various divine and demonic warriors. Key events include the demon Andhaka's attempt to deceive Parvati by assuming Shiva's form, Parvati's escape and transformation into a plant, and the eventual defeat of the demons through the combined efforts of Vishnu, Brahma, and Indra.

beats.title: Indra's Battle with Jambha and the Divine Charioteer
beats.summary: Indra, after his vajra is shattered by Jambha, seeks refuge in Vishnu, who directs him to Agni for a new weapon. Agni gives him a divine śakti. Indra fights Jambha, but his elephant is killed and he falls. Gandharvas provide a chariot, but Indra needs a charioteer. A sage's son, Mātali, born from the sage's wife's prophecy, becomes his charioteer. Indra then attacks the demon army, and Vishnu kills Kujambha. Jambha attacks Indra, who uses the śakti, but Jambha strikes it with his mace.

beats.title: Slaying of Jambha
beats.summary: Jambha is struck in the heart by a divine weapon and falls unconscious. The daityas flee in fear. The gaṇas rejoice and praise Hari and Indra. The chapter ends with Pulastya speaking.

beats.title: The Slaying of Andhaka and the Origin of Bhairava, Carcika, and Mars
beats.summary: Andhaka, the demon son of Hiranyaksha, attacks Shiva with a massive army. After a fierce battle, Shiva manifests the Bhairava form, slays Andhaka's horses and chariot, and impales Andhaka on his trident. From drops of Shiva's sweat and Andhaka's blood, the goddess Carcika and a boy are born. Shiva grants boons to Carcika and to the planet Mars (Kuja). Andhaka, purified by Shiva's third-eye fire, realizes Shiva's supremacy and offers a hymn of praise.

beats.title: The Liberation of Andhaka and His Transformation into Bhṛṅgī
beats.summary: Andhaka, a demon king, repents his sins and praises Shiva, who grants him liberation from his demonic nature and makes him a gaṇa named Bhṛṅgī. Shiva then presents Bhṛṅgī to Pārvatī, who also grants him a boon. The chapter concludes with the gaṇas returning to their abodes.

beats.title: The Origin of the Maruts and Indra's Epithet Gotrabhit
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about Indra's epithet 'Gotrabhit' (mountain-splitter). Pulastya narrates how Indra, sent by Shiva to the Malaya mountain, defeated the demon Paka and others, earning names like Paka-shasana and Purandara. Then he explains the origin of the Maruts: Diti, after losing her sons, sought a son to kill Indra. Kaśyapa advised her to observe a vow of purity for a thousand divine years. Indra served her, seeking a flaw. After 1000 years, Diti fell asleep with her hair touching her feet, breaking purity. Indra entered her womb, saw the fetus, and cut it into seven pieces with his vajra, then each into seven again, creating the 49 Maruts. Diti accepted this as fate, and the Maruts became Indra's servants. Thus Indra became known as Gotrabhit.

beats.title: Origins of the Maruts Across Three Manvantaras
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the origins of the Maruts across three Manvantaras: Svayambhuva, Svārociṣa, and Uttama. In each, a childless king and his devoted queen undergo trials, leading to the birth of seven sons who become Maruts.

beats.title: Origins of the Maruts
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the origins of the Maruts (wind gods) across three different epochs: first from King Citragu's self-sacrifice in fire, then from the sage Raivata's daughter's suicide, and finally from sage Maṅkaṇaka's spilled semen in the Sarasvatī river. In each case, the Maruts are born from fire or water and are adopted by Brahmā.

beats.title: The Battle of Devas and Asuras: Bali's Rise and Fall
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the story of the battle between the devas and asuras, focusing on the role of Bali, Kalanemi, and Vishnu. The asuras, led by Bali and Kalanemi, initially overpower the devas, but Vishnu intervenes, defeats Kalanemi, and causes the asuras to flee.

beats.title: Bali's Conquest of Heaven and Prahlada's Counsel
beats.summary: After the demon Bana's defeat, the demons regroup and attack the gods. Vishnu advises the gods to fight, then disappears. Shukra informs Bali that Vishnu has left, encouraging him to attack. Bali and Bana lead a fierce assault, causing the gods to flee to Brahma's realm. Bali takes over heaven, assigning demons to the roles of various gods. Prahlada visits Bali, who offers him the throne, but Prahlada declines and advises Bali on righteous rule, emphasizing the importance of serving Brahmins and maintaining the varna system.

beats.title: Bali's Righteous Reign and the Visit of Shri
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates how after the gods returned to Brahmaloka, Bali ruled the three worlds righteously. Kali, seeing the world full of dharma, sought refuge in Brahma, who explained that only Vishnu can stop Bali. Kali then went to the Vibhitaka forest. Under Bali's rule, dharma flourished with four feet. The goddess Shri, in four forms (white, red, yellow, blue), approached Bali, explaining her association with different varnas and the four treasures (Mahapadma, Padma, Mahanila, Shankha). She praised Bali's valor and entered him, followed by other goddesses like Hri, Shri, Dhiti, etc. The chapter ends with a description of Bali's virtuous rule.

beats.title: Indra's Penance and Vishnu's Promise to Aditi
beats.summary: Indra, having lost his kingdom to Bali, seeks guidance from Brahma and Kashyapa. They advise him to seek refuge in Vishnu. Indra performs penance at a sacred place near the rivers Mandakini and Ganga, where Vishnu as Gadadhara appears and forgives his sin of fetal murder. Indra then returns to his mother Aditi, who, distressed by his defeat, undertakes a vow to worship Vishnu. Vishnu appears and promises to incarnate as her son to destroy the demons.

beats.title: Prahlāda's Curse on Bali and Advice to Surrender
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Nārada how the demons lost their tejas due to Viṣṇu's presence in Aditi's womb as Vāmana. Bali questions Prahlāda about the cause, and Prahlāda explains that Viṣṇu has incarnated to protect the devas. Bali arrogantly dismisses Viṣṇu's power, leading Prahlāda to curse him to lose his kingdom. Bali repents, and Prahlāda advises him to surrender to Viṣṇu.

beats.title: Narada's Advice to Bali and His Pilgrimage
beats.summary: Narada continues his discourse, advising the demon king Bali to take refuge in Vishnu, the eternal lord, who is the support for those fallen in the cycle of samsara. Bali is encouraged to become a devotee of Janardana (Vishnu) for his own welfare. Narada then resolves to undertake a pilgrimage to holy places to atone for his sins, after which he will go to the abode of Acyuta (Vishnu) in the form of Narasimha. Pulastya narrates that after consoling Bali and remembering the lord of yoga, Vishnu, Narada took leave of the demon chiefs and departed for his pilgrimage.

beats.title: Prahlada's Pilgrimage and the Deception of Dhundhu
beats.summary: Narada asks Pulastya about the tirthas visited by Prahlada. Pulastya describes Prahlada's pilgrimage to various sacred sites, including Manasa, Kacchapa, Krsna, Gajasahvaya, and Yamuna, where he worshipped Vishnu in various forms. The narrative then shifts to the story of the demon Dhundhu, who, after receiving a boon from Brahma, conquers the heavens and plans to perform a hundred horse sacrifices to attain Brahmaloka. The gods, led by Indra, seek Vishnu's help. Vishnu assumes the form of a dwarf (Vamana) and floats in the Devika river, waiting to deceive Dhundhu.

beats.title: Vamana Subdues Dhundhu
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Vamana, the dwarf avatar of Vishnu, who asks the demon king Dhundhu for three paces of land, then expands to cosmic size, covering the earth and sky in two steps, and with the third step pushes Dhundhu into a pit, covering him with sand. The demon is thus subdued, and Indra regains heaven.

beats.title: The Ghost-King's Tale: The Power of a Single Gift
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the story of a merchant named Sudharma who, after being robbed in a desert, meets a ghost-king who was formerly a miserly brahmin named Somaśarman. The ghost-king explains that his current state is due to his miserliness, but he once gave a gift of food and water on the Ekadashi of Shravana Dvadashi, which now sustains him and his ghostly followers.

beats.title: The Power of Shravana-dvadashi
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of a merchant's son who, after encountering a pretaraja (king of ghosts), performs the Shravana-dvadashi vow and offers pindas in Gaya, leading to liberation for the ghosts and his own eventual rebirth as a handsome brahmin, then king Pururavas, through the grace of the Lord.

beats.title: Nakshatra Purusha Vrata
beats.summary: Pulastya describes to Narada the Nakshatra Purusha Vrata, a ritual worship of Vishnu as the embodiment of the 27 lunar mansions (nakshatras), detailing the correspondence between each nakshatra and a part of Vishnu's body, the procedure for worship including offerings (dohada) and brahmana feeding, and the benefits such as beauty, health, and destruction of sins. Examples of past practitioners include Bhrigu, Arundhati, Aditya (who obtained Revanta), Rambha, Menaka, and King Pururavas.

beats.title: Prahlada's Pilgrimage and the Slaying of Jalodbhava
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada the pilgrimage of Prahlada, the king of demons, to various sacred tirthas in Kurukṣetra, culminating in the story of the demon Jalodbhava who was slain by Viṣṇu and Śiva exchanging weapons. The chapter highlights the power of tirthas and the unity of Viṣṇu and Śiva.

beats.title: The Story of the Sudarshana Chakra and Upamanyu's Devotion
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the story of how Lord Vishnu obtained the Sudarshana chakra from Shiva after performing severe penance, and how the demon Shridama was slain. The chapter also includes the story of the sage Upamanyu, who, after being advised by his mother to worship Virupaksha (Shiva), obtained milk to drink.

beats.title: Prahlada's Pilgrimage
beats.summary: This chapter describes the pilgrimage of the demon Prahlada to various sacred tirthas across India, where he bathes, worships deities, and performs rites for ancestors. The journey culminates at Rudrakoti, where Shiva manifests in multiple forms to satisfy sages, and continues to other holy sites.

beats.title: Prahlada's Pilgrimage
beats.summary: The chapter describes the pilgrimage of Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, as he travels to various sacred sites, worships deities, and performs austerities. It highlights his devotion and the purifying power of these holy places.

beats.title: Gajendra's Deliverance
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the story of Gajendra's deliverance. He describes the Trikuta mountain, its three peaks, and the lake where Gajendra, the elephant king, is seized by a crocodile. In distress, Gajendra prays to Lord Vishnu, reciting a hymn of surrender. The chapter includes the full hymn (Gajendra Stuti) praising Vishnu's various forms and attributes.

beats.title: Gajendramoksha: The Liberation of the Elephant King
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Gajendra's liberation (Gajendramoksha). The elephant king Gajendra, while bathing in a lake, is seized by a crocodile. After a long struggle, he prays to Lord Vishnu, who rushes to his aid on Garuda, kills the crocodile with his discus, and liberates both Gajendra and the crocodile (who was a cursed Gandharva named Huhu). The chapter concludes with the benefits of reciting this story and a hymn to Vishnu.

beats.title: The Rākṣasa's Repentance and the Brahmin Boy's Cleverness
beats.summary: A brahmin sage performs a protective meditation (Viṣṇu Pañjara) that subdues a violent rākṣasa. The rākṣasa, repenting his sins, seeks liberation. The sage directs him to other brahmins, but the rākṣasa later captures a brahmin boy. The boy negotiates his release by promising to return after offering fruits to his guru, and the rākṣasa agrees on condition that the boy teaches him how to attain liberation from his sinful life.

beats.title: The Brahmin's Son and the Rakshasa
beats.summary: A brahmin's son is captured by a rakshasa. He prays to Agni and Sarasvati appears, instructing him to recite a hymn to Vishnu. The brahmin chants a long hymn praising Vishnu's various forms and attributes, seeking forgiveness for sins. The chapter concludes by stating that reciting this hymn destroys sins accumulated over seven births.

beats.title: The Power of the Sarasvata Stotra
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the story of a Brahmin who, while being devoured by a Rakshasa, recites the Sarasvata Stotra of Vishnu, which was originally taught by Sarasvati to Agni and then to the Brahmin. The Rakshasa is freed from a curse, performs penance, and attains Vishnu's abode. The chapter extols the power of this stotra to destroy sins.

beats.title: The Hymn of Vishnu's Universal Form
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Vishnu (also identified with Shiva and Brahman) recited by Pulastya. It extols the deity's manifold forms, cosmic functions, and attributes, and concludes with the story of Rudra being freed from sin by reciting this hymn at Varanasi.

beats.title: The Sin-Destroying Hymn of Vishnu and Shiva
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates a hymn (stava) that destroys sin, listing numerous names and forms of deities, primarily Vishnu and Shiva, as well as sages and other beings. The hymn is said to be purifying, bestowing merit, and destroying great sins through recitation, remembrance, or hearing.

beats.title: The Birth and Education of Vamana
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to Narada how the sage Atreya and others, disturbed by the presence of the demon king Bali's sacrifice at Kurukshetra, travel northwards and eventually settle at a sacred forest where the four-faced Vishnu (caturmurti) is established. Meanwhile, Shukracharya conducts Bali's horse sacrifice. The goddess Aditi gives birth to Vamana, an avatar of Vishnu. Brahma and sages praise Vamana, who undergoes rites of passage and quickly masters the Vedas. Vamana then asks his guru Bharadvaja for permission to go to Bali's sacrifice, and Bharadvaja inquires about Vishnu's eternal abodes.

beats.title: Vishnu's All-Pervasive Nature
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lord Vishnu (as Vamana) describes his all-pervasive nature, stating that he resides in various sacred places through his incarnations. He explains that all beings, from gods to immovable objects, are born from him and that merely seeing or chanting his forms destroys sin.

beats.title: Vishnu's Abodes and Forms
beats.summary: This chapter is a dialogue between the sage Pulastya and the sage Bharadvaja, where Vishnu enumerates his various abodes and forms across different regions, worlds, and realms, emphasizing the sanctity and power of these places for liberation.

beats.title: Bali's Dilemma and the Story of Nishakara
beats.summary: Bali asks Shukra why the earth trembles, the oceans are agitated, and the sacrificial offerings are not accepted. Shukra explains that Vasudeva (Krishna) is approaching, causing these disturbances. Bali then asks what should be done, and Shukra advises that nothing can be given or said to Krishna, who contains the entire universe. Bali disagrees and tells the story of the sage Kosakara and his son Nishakara to illustrate the power of past impressions and the importance of proper conduct.

beats.title: The Tale of Niśākara's Past Lives
beats.summary: A sage named Niśākara recounts his past lives to his parents, detailing a series of births and deaths due to his past karmas, including pride, adultery, and violence. He describes being born as a tiger, donkey, parrot, bull, and finally as their son, remembering his previous lives and the suffering in hell.

beats.title: Divakirti's Renunciation and Bali's Discourse on Past-Life Impressions
beats.summary: Bali recounts to Shukra how his son, Divakirti, remembering his past life, renounces household life and goes to Badari ashram to meditate on Narayana. Bali explains that past-life impressions (abhyasa) drive actions, and that even study, charity, and austerities can be binding if done mechanically. He advises Shukra to guide Divakirti into household dharma, but Divakirti departs for the forest.

beats.title: Vamana Avatar and the Three Steps
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the Vamana avatar story: Lord Vishnu as a dwarf brahmin approaches King Bali during a sacrifice, asks for three paces of land, Bali grants it despite warnings, Vishnu expands to cosmic form covering earth and heaven, then sends Bali to Sutala, promising him Indra-ship in a future manvantara.

beats.title: Bali's Boon and the Future Festivals
beats.summary: Trivikrama (Vamana) grants Bali the boon of ruling Sutala and foretells the future festival of Dvarapratipada. He then returns to heaven, and the asuras Shalva, Maya, and Bana establish their respective cities. The chapter concludes with the praise of Vamana's incarnation.

beats.title: Vishnu's Vamana Form in Brahma's Abode
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates to the sage how Lord Vishnu, after binding Bali and giving the heavens to Indra, disappeared. He then went to Brahma's abode, where Brahma praised him with a long hymn. Pleased, Vishnu granted Brahma a boon, and Brahma requested that Vishnu stay in his abode in his Vamana form. Vishnu agreed and remained there, worshipped by Brahma and Indra. The chapter ends with Pulastya promising to tell what Bali did in Rasatala.

beats.title: Bali's Distress and Prahlada's Teaching on Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: Bali, the demon king, resides in Sutala with his wife Vindhyavali. Vishnu's discus Sudarshana enters Patala, causing turmoil. Bali worships it and prays for purification. After the discus leaves, Bali becomes distressed and summons his grandfather Prahlada, who teaches him about the supremacy of Vishnu and the path of devotion.

beats.title: The Glory of Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Vamana Purana emphasizes the supreme importance of devotion (bhakti) to Vishnu (Madhusudana, Janardana, Narayana, Krishna). It declares that without devotion, all rituals, sacrifices, knowledge, and austerities are futile. The chapter extols the power of chanting the mantra 'Namo Narayanaya', remembering Vishnu's names, and performing worship with faith. It contrasts the efficacy of devotion with other practices, stating that even those who worship other deities but ultimately honor Kesava attain merit. The text encourages constant meditation, remembrance, and glorification of Vishnu as the path to liberation from samsara.

beats.title: Prahlada's Instructions on Worship and the Example of King Jyāmagha
beats.summary: Prahlada instructs the demon king Bali on the proper methods of worshiping Vishnu, including specific flowers, leaves, incense, offerings, and donations for each month. He emphasizes the importance of building temples and serving brahmins, citing the example of King Jyāmagha who attained liberation through such devotion.

beats.title: Bali's Devotion and the Value of Elders' Words
beats.summary: After the departure of the delighted grandfather (Brahma), the demon king Bali, with his wife Vindhyavali, performed worship in the temple built by Vishvakarma. He engaged in cleaning, anointing, offering barley and sugar, and lighting lamps. He also arranged for the recitation of Puranas by learned Brahmins. Lord Janardana, assuming a divine form, stood guard at the door with a mace, preventing the entry of enemies. Bali, devotedly worshiping Hari's lotus feet, remembered his words and developed humility. He recited the story and extolled the value of elders' words, comparing them to medicine for those bitten by the snake of adversity, and emphasizing that following elders' advice leads to success, as exemplified by Bali himself.

beats.title: The Glories of the Vamana Purana
beats.summary: Pulastya tells Narada the glories of reciting and hearing the Vamana Purana, describing the immense spiritual benefits, including destruction of sins, attainment of various sacrificial rewards, and ultimately liberation. He emphasizes the secrecy of this teaching and the importance of devotion to Vishnu.
